dsls.markdown: # Teaching Generative AI #### Welcome! This professional learning module is designed to help you prepare to teach Code.org's **Exploring Generative AI** course. #### This course is designed for 8th-12th grade students and consists of two units: - #### ***Unit 1: Foundations of Generative AI*** - #### ***Unit 2: Customizing Language Models*** #### It can be taught as a complete course or as separate units, offering flexibility to meet your educational needs. ## **In this module, you will:** - #### Get an introduction to teaching both units in the Exploring Generative AI course - #### Define and identify different uses and purposes of Generative AI - #### Understand the ethical impact of Generative AI - #### Sample lessons from the Exploring Generative AI course ### **Suggested total time:** 2 hours ### **Lesson one suggested time:** 20 minutes ## Take the pre-survey #### Code.org values and is responsive to feedback. Please fill out this short pre-survey to tell us about who you are and how we can best support you. Take the pre-survey ## Let's get started!

# Receiving a Certificate for this Module #### Upon finishing this module, you will be eligible to receive a certificate of completion similar to the one above. You must complete **every level** in this module to access your certificate. #### We encourage you to share your certificates to social media to celebrate your success! ## FAQ ### **How do I complete a level in a self-paced module?** #### Completion varies by level type: * #### On a text-only level (such as this one), you'll need to click the "Continue" button. * #### For an AI Chat level, you may be asked to complete a task. Some levels will require a correct answer. You will have an unlimited number of attempts. * #### For a checking for understanding level, such as vocabulary matching or multiple choice, you will need to answer correctly. You will have an unlimited number of attempts. * #### For an open reflection level, you will need to submit an answer to the question(s) provided.
### **When will I receive my certificate?** #### You'll be taken to a congratulations page once you've completed every level within a self-paced experience. If the self-paced experience has multiple units (see the image directly below), you will see the congratulations page once every level is complete in every unit. #### This self-paced experience has two units: Getting Started with Code.org and Exploring Generative AI.
### **Where can I find the certificates I've earned?** #### You can find all your certificates at studio.code.org/my-professional-learning. Your self-paced certificates will be in the “My Self Paced Professional Learning Courses” section in the Actions column once you have completed the module. #### You can also access your certificates by clicking "Print Certificate" on each module overview page. ### **Why can't I access my certificate?** #### Ensure that *every* level has been completed in the module. Your module overview page should look similar to this image: #### You may need to return to individual levels to complete them. If every level is complete and you still can't access a certificate, send us a message.

# Exploring Generative AI Course Overview #### The Exploring Generative AI course is designed as a standalone unit for 8-12 students that can be integrated into any existing Computer Science or STEM curriculum, offering teachers the flexibility to introduce these concepts according to their schedule and educational needs. #### This course consists of two units. You can choose to teach one or both units, whichever works for your class. ## Unit 1: Foundations of Generative AI #### This unit consists of 11 lessons and takes about 2.5 weeks to complete. It is primarily unplugged and can be done without devices. ### **Students will:** - #### Understand the **fundamental concepts** generative AI. - #### Demystify generative AI models by exploring their internal structures through input, storage, process, and output. - #### Understand the impact of training data on model performance and the potential for bias in generative AI systems. #### Explore Unit One here ## Unit 2: Customizing Language Models #### This unit consists of 15 lessons and takes about 4 weeks to complete and features the new AI Chat lab tool. ### **Students will:** - #### Engage in hands-on activities that involve **exploring, customizing, and creating AI bots**. - #### Explore and discuss the ethical implications and responsibilities associated generataive AI. - #### Design and build a personalized chatbot, adjusting key settings, and create a model card to share their project for peer interaction. #### Explore Unit Two here

# AI Literacy #### As AI rapidly transforms our world, it's essential to equip students with a solid understanding of how AI works and how to effectively interact with it. Organizations like AI4K12, CSTA, and Teach AI are leading efforts to provide schools and teachers with resources to prepare students for the future. ## 5 Big Ideas #### These guidelines, from AI4K12 ensure that students engage with AI in a responsible and ethical manner and the **Exploring Generative AI** Curriculum aligns with these guidelines, offering a comprehensive approach to teaching students about AI.
Computers interpret the world through sensors, a process known as perception, which involves extracting meaning from sensory signals.
Agents use representations of the world for reasoning, a core challenge in both natural and artificial intelligence. While AI can handle complex reasoning tasks, it does so differently than human thought processes.
Computers learn from data through machine learning, which identifies patterns using statistical inference. Recent advances in AI are driven by learning algorithms that develop new representations, although they require large amounts of training data, often provided by people.
Intelligent agents need extensive knowledge to interact naturally with humans, including language comprehension, emotion recognition, and understanding cultural norms. While current AI systems can handle language to some degree, they lack the general reasoning and conversational skills of even a young child.
AI significantly influences society by transforming work, travel, communication, and caregiving, but it can also introduce risks like biased data impacting fairness. Therefore, it's crucial to address AI's societal effects and establish ethical guidelines for its design and use.

# Ethical Approach #### When learning about AI, it is crucial to integrate discussions on ethics due to the profound societal impacts and ethical dilemmas the technology presents. This curriculum weaves ethical considerations throughout, rather than relegating ethics to a single lesson. #### By embedding ethical discussions, case studies, and extension opportunities into the learning experience, students gain a deeper, more nuanced understanding of AI's implications, encouraging critical thinking and responsible innovation. ## Leaders Framework #### LEADERS is an acronym representing key principles designed to guide lessons and discussions about the societal impacts of AI, including bias and ethical concerns. These principles provide reflective practices to help educators better prepare for these important conversations.
L Link to your subject area
E Errors occur for many reasons
A Assume someone in the room is your datapoint
D Data sources can be biased
E Empathy for multiple stakeholders
R Real-world cases are history, not opinion
S Show solutions!
### **Read more about the Leaders Framework in this** Teacher Guide. ## Embedded Ethical Opportunities in the Curriculum #### Throughout the curriculum you will notice callouts in the lesson plan and slides depicting opportunities for ethical discussions.

Teacher Tip: As technology evolves and new stories emerge, consider incorporating your own real-world examples into the lessons.

- #### **Real-World Connections** - These showcase real-world case studies or individuals addressing AI's impact on culture or society, providing students with practical connections to lesson content. - #### **Pause and Reflect** - These prompt students to reflect on their own experiences through scenarios related to recent activities or concepts. They offer a chance to pause, discuss, and explore personal opinions on technology, with examples provided to guide the conversation and encourage students to develop or refine their ethical views on AI. #### Green tabs marked **'Ethics Opportunities'** are embedded in the lesson plan. ![](https://images.code.org/042f338730fcc4da6ccc7917bc4d0b1e-image-1726684253906.png) ### **Read more about Ethical Opportunities in this** Teacher Guide.

# Lesson Resources #### Everything you need to deliver engaging and effective classes is included with each **lesson plan** to help make your teaching experience as seamless as possible. ## Each lesson includes:
1. Clear Objectives

Statements outline the specific knowledge and skills students will gain by the end of each lesson.

2. Detailed teaching guides

Step-by-step guides walks you through each lesson, including teacher tips to ensure smooth delivery and maximize student learning.

3. Engaging slides

Visually appealing presentations keep students focused and provide a clear structure for the lesson.

4. Interactive student handouts

Handouts offer activities, practice problems, and note-taking guides to reinforce key concepts.

![]()

# ***New*** Lesson Features #### This course includes new features designed to enhance and streamline your teaching experience. ## Lesson Plans #### These lesson plans have images of slides embedded to make it easier for you to follow along with the lesson. #### See example lesson
## Slide Decks #### The slide decks for this unit include embedded lesson plans in the speaker note section of the slide for easy access, allowing you to choose the most convenient format to guide your students through the lesson. #### See example slide deck
## Student levels #### The student levels are at the top of the lesson plan instead of embedded throughout to make it easier for you to explore the student experience. #### See example lesson plan
## Ethical Opportunities #### In addition to teacher tips and assessment callouts, this unit introduces ethical opportunity callouts (in green) to help guide discussions on ethical scenarios. Engaging in these conversations with your class fosters critical thinking and prepares students to navigate ethical challenges in the real world. #### See example lesson plan

# Navigating this Module #### 🎉 Congratulations on completing this lesson.
📝 Engage with our Learning Community!

####Participating in the Gen AI Self-Paced PL Learning Community is optional and not necessary to earn a certificate for this module. However, we encourage you to engage with the broader Gen AI learner community. #### **Please share your motivation for teaching the Exploring Generative AI course.**

#### **What's next?** This professional learning module includes three additional lessons to enhance your understanding and teaching of the Exploring Generative AI course. - #### **Lesson 1: Introduction to Teaching Exploring Generative AI**- This lesson gives an overview of the curriculum features in Exploring Generative AI. #### ➡️ You are here! - #### **Lesson 2: Foundations of Generative AI** - This lesson covers basic concepts on Generative AI introduced in the first unit of the course. - #### **Lesson 3: Customizing Large Language Models** - This lesson covers the second unit in the Exploring Generative AI course and focuses on practicing skills for the hands-on activities that involve exploring, customizing, and creating AI bots. ## Let's continue this exciting journey!

# Foundations of Generative AI #### This unit aims to build a foundational understanding of text-based generative AI models, focusing on core concepts over technical skills. #### Students will demystify generative AI models by exploring their internal structures through the familiar lens of input, storage, process, and output. They will gain insights into how these models represent language, the impact of training data on model performance, and the potential for bias. #### Using this knowledge they will be presented with scenarios throughout the unit where they can help educate individuals who feel powerless or lack agency in how AI is impacting their lives, or respond to individuals who have only read the hype headlines and offer feedback or criticism based on their knowledge of how these AI systems work. #### Let's start by build a foundational understanding of text-based generative AI models, focusing on core concepts before we dive into technical skills. ## **In this lesson you will:** - #### Review the content of each lesson in the Foundations of Generative AI Unit - #### Preview activities in the lessons ### **Suggested time:** 40 minutes

# What is Generative AI? #### Generative AI is a type of artificial intelligence that can create new content. In this unit, we will be exploring Large Language Models, or LLMs. - #### **Artificial Intelligence:** A technology that mimics human intelligence, performing tasks such as understanding language, recognizing patterns, and making decisions. - #### **Large Language Model:** A computer program that analyzes large amounts of raw human-created data to the point where it can represent and recreate new data to perform a variety of tasks
--- ## Why Students Should Learn It: #### It's important for students to learn how generative AI works because it helps them understand the technology shaping our world, fostering critical thinking about its capabilities and limitations. #### Many students have already encountered AI in tools like search engines, social media, and voice assistants. #### Additionally, this knowledge empowers them to use AI responsibly and creatively in their future careers. #### The **Exploring Generative AI** course aligns with the CSTA supported AI4K12 initiative, ensuring students gain a comprehensive understanding of AI concepts, preparing them to navigate and contribute to an AI-driven future.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### In the Foundations of Generative AI unit, students will learn the foundations of Large Language Models. Then, in the Customizing Large Language Models Unit, they will work on the Code.org platform to customize and create one. LLMs can also be referred to as ChatBots. You may be familiar with models such as ChatGPT.

## What is an LLM?

#### A large language model, or LLM, is a type of artificial intelligence system designed to understand, generate, and work with human language at a vast scale.

#### It's trained on a wide array of text data and learning patterns from an extensive database. This training enables it to perform tasks like answering questions, writing text, and translating languages, based on the context and information it has learned.

* * *

#### Watch this video clip to hear Mira Murati from Open AI and Cristobal Valenzuela from Runway explain how large language models work.

answers.text: A type of calculator that can perform complex mathematical operations.
A software program designed to edit and enhance images.
A computer program trained to understand and generate human language.
A robotic device used for manufacturing and assembly lines

questions.text: Which of the following best describes a Large Language Model (LLM)?

dsls.markdown:
# Introduction to Generative AI ## Lesson Highlight: Graphic Organizer #### In Lesson 1, students are introduced to the **Input, Storage, Processing, Output** (ISPO) framework to understand how generative AI works. In Lesson 1, students are provided with a graphic organizer which they add to throughout the unit. #### Before you move on, check out this exemplar Graphic Organizer to help you understand the ISPO framework. This exemplar is available in the Lesson Plan.

Teacher Tip: Managing vocabulary throughout this unit can be challenging. Consider using additional teaching strategies like word walls or vocabulary handouts to help students consistently track and reference important concepts.

## Lesson Preview #### Click on the expandable box below to learn more about Lesson 1, including the lesson overview, key learning objectives, and to review the full Lesson Plan. Take as much time as you'd like to review this lesson before moving on.
🔎 Lesson 1: Introduction to Generative AI

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we begin to understand Generative AI's impact on how we live and work? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson introduces the learning goals focused on artificial intelligence (AI), transitioning into generative AI. It begins with a discussion on the impact of past technological innovations on society. Students then explore the concept of generative AI through the lens of someone familiar only with its hype, aiming to foster understanding and responsible engagement with AI technology. A key part of the lesson is the introduction of the Input, Storage, Processing, Output (ISPO) framework, essential for understanding generative AI's workings. The lesson concludes with students receiving a graphic organizer for the unit and sharing their anticipations about learning more on generative AI. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Distinguish between different types of AI, including machine learning, deep learning, and generative AI. * #### Evaluate the potential impact of generative AI on society and discuss its capabilities and limitations. * #### Identify and explain the four key components that make up a computer system. ### Full Lesson Plan

# Input ## Lesson Highlight: Youtube Playlist #### Key learning topics are reinforced throughout this unit in short (2-3 minute) videos. Keep an eye out for these as you preview the lesson plans. Watching these videos yourself are also a great way to reinforce your understanding of Generative AI. #### Check out the whole video series here. #### Throughout the next few levels, we'll spend some time diving into the lessons and associated activities connected to the topics of each of these videos - **Input, Embeddings, Neural Networks, Attention, and Output**. Come back to this videos if you have trouble understanding the content of the lessons. #### In the **input** step, a language model is provided with large amounts of training data. The model then learns to recognize words and understand language rules by analyzing this data through a pre-training process. ## Lesson Preview
🔎 Lesson 2: Input & Training Data

#### **Question of the Day:** How does a computer recognize words used in language? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students explore the process of recognizing and categorizing words, comparing it to how AI systems achieve similar tasks. Through discussion and activities, they consider the data sources an AI system requires to build a task-specific vocabulary, leading to the understanding that language models necessitate vast data amounts, like the entire internet. The lesson concludes with students updating a graphic organizer to reflect the Input step of generative AI. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain how a language model uses data to recognize words as input * #### Identify potential data sources relevant to a task being performed by a language model ### Full Lesson Plan

# Bias ## Lesson Highlight: Teaching about Bias #### In Lesson 3 students are introduced to the topic of **bias** in AI. #### Bias occurs when a decision favors some things and de-prioritizes or excludes others. Bias within an AI system is a direct reflection of the data a system was trained on and the way it is trained. #### Teaching about biases in technology can be a sensitive subject for your students. These **action items** will help you feel more comfortable discussing these issues in your class.
🖥️Assume all AI systems have bias

Even AI systems that seem innocuous are likely to have biases, whether those are deeply harmful or just irritating.

Before using an AI system to make decisions in your classroom, examine the ways it might be biased. Start by trying to understand the historical biases in the data the AI system uses.

🚦Approach discussions of AI bias carefully

Many students have had personal experiences with bias, but may not have used that specific word to explain it. Approach discussions of AI bias with an understanding that students may need time to process such an emotionally difficult concept.

🌍 Emphasize the importance of team diversity

Talk with students about how team diversity can help in developing widely useful AI systems. Use this fact as a motivator to encourage students to design for their community and advocate for their wants and needs. Avoid positioning students as the ones to fix others' mistakes, but rather as innovators with a deeply valuable perspective.

✅ Support companies committed to reducing bias

Seek out companies that follow specific, actionable commitments that reduce bias in their AI tools. Read about the company's background, checking for independent audits and partnerships with reputable community organizations.

#### Addressing concerns about AI bias from administrators, parents, or fellow teachers can be challenging. To facilitate these conversations, utilize the range of **scripts** provided in this link. These resources are designed to help **clarify issues and foster understanding**. Additionally, there is a specific script available for students to help them express their experiences when affected by AI bias. ## Lesson Preview
🔎 Lesson 3: Bias in the Machine

#### **Question of the Day:** How do our choices about input data influence a large language model? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson formally introduces the concept of bias, where certain information is favored over others. Students experience how bias can implicitly and unintentionally creep into large-language models by participating in an activity where they are asked to source articles and information for a RecipeBot. Once they've used search engine results to generate their training data, they analyze patterns in the data that the class found as a collection, noticing unintended biases such as certain cuisines being unintentionally excluded or all the articles falling within a certain narrow date range. After acknowledging how bias can appear, students analyze several scenarios and discuss how bias could appear in the training data of each one. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Describe patterns in collections of data sources that contribute to bias ### Full Lesson Plan

# Embeddings ## Lesson Highlight: Character Quiz Activity #### During **Lesson 4: Understanding Embeddings**, students investigate how characters from stories or movies can be similar as a way to understand how words can be similar. #### Students do the following... * #### Create a representation of chosen characters based on their different traits. * #### Collect data on other characters by sharing with classmates. * #### Compare two characters row-by-row, noticing when dots are close together or far apart. For example, Kim and Hawa's characters are on opposite ends of the good/bad spectrum, but are close in several other areas. #### During discussions, students will eventually understand that they can compare numbers in each position to determine how similar or different characters are. One powerful insight is that it doesn't matter what each position represents, only how close or far the numbers are in that same position. #### These key concepts helps us generalize the idea of “character traits” to **embeddings** which are the set of numbers used to represent a word in a large-language model. ## Lesson Previews
🔎 Lesson 4: Understanding Embeddings

#### **Question of the Day:** How do computers represent the meanings of words? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students explore how language models understand and represent word meanings. Beginning with a game, they investigate the concept of words being "close" in meaning. This leads to an activity where students assume the roles of fictional characters, using a sequence of numbers to represent their "character traits" and comparing these numerical representations to others. The lesson demonstrates that language models use a similar method, known as embeddings, to represent words as sequences of numbers, with similar words being numerically close to each other. The lesson concludes with students updating the storage section of their graphic organizer to encapsulate the idea of word embeddings. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Describe how embeddings show the similarity or difference between two items * #### Explain how language models use embeddings to store the meaning of wordss ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 5: Embeddings: How They're Created

#### **Question of the Day:** How do language models learn the embeddings they use to represent words? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students delve into the creation of word embeddings. They start by interpreting a passage in a made-up language, using context clues to understand word meanings based on their relationships with other words. Following this, they engage in an activity to construct embeddings from sentences by noting word proximity. The climax reveals that students worked with two distinct passages, illustrating how the same word can have different representations based on its usage context. This insight sets the stage for upcoming lessons on how language models use sentence structure and context to determine word meanings accurately. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Construct an embedding for a text passage * #### Explain how different passages can create different embeddings ### Full Lesson Plan

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
# Neural Networks ## Lesson Highlight: Neural Networks Activity #### During **Lesson 6: Neural Networks**, students help a computer design meals for an elementary school cafeteria. #### 📝 **Try out this activity on your own**. Open up the slide deck for this lesson, and work through the activity. You can print or draw the Neural Networks - Activity Guide (shown to the right). #### Make sure you read the presenter notes in the slides as you work, and consider reviewing the lesson plan to get the full context for the activity. #### When you're done, 🧠 **reflect on how the activity went below.** What was your major take-away?

Teacher Tip: Students will be drawing their own neural network and iterating on the design. This means students will be doing some trial and error, which will involve lots of erasing. If your classroom has them available, consider using whiteboards for this activity instead of the activity guide so students can quickly and easily erase their work as the lesson progresses.

## Lesson Previews
🔎 Lesson 6: Understanding Neural Networks

#### **Question of the Day:** How do neural networks make decisions? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson introduces neural networks through a hands-on activity where students create a lunch recommender for a school cafeteria. It starts with an exercise involving two mad-libs that use the same words in different orders, highlighting how word relations and grammar influence word choice. Students then work together to build a neural network model for the cafeteria's lunch choices, learning about key concepts like hidden layers and weights. The lesson concludes by tying these ideas back to language, prompting students to think about how words can change meaning in different contexts or phrases. #### **Students will be able to:** - #### Construct a neural network to represent a situation and make decisions based on inputs ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 7: Neural Networks: How They're Trained

#### **Question of the Day:** How are neural networks trained to represent relationships between words? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson explores supervised learning and its role in teaching neural networks language structure. Starting with a game, students learn through trial and error and use their past outcomes to inform future decisions. They then extend this approach to engaging in conversations in a fictional language, initially facing more failures but gradually improving as they identify patterns. This process illustrates how neural networks adjust their behavior based on new data. The lesson concludes with a reflection on whether this process signifies true "intelligence" or merely a sophisticated rule-following system. #### **Students will be able to:** - #### Use trial-and-error and past data to increase their accuracy in a supervised learning process ### Full Lesson Plan

## Reflection #### How did the activity go? What was your major take-away?

# Attention ## Lesson Highlight: Activity Overview Videos #### In **Lesson 8: Attention Is All You Need** students explore how words can have multiple meanings, and how both humans and Gen AI determine the intended meaning based on context clues in the sentence. #### This lesson, as well as a few others in this Unit, has an **Activity Overview Video** to help you visualize how the activity will look before you try it with students. #### Watch the video below to preview the Meaning from Context Activity. You may want to also open up the Activity Guide and/or Lesson Plan to follow along with the video. #### You'll find the links to these videos in the _For the teachers_ section of the Lesson Plan. ## Lesson Preview
🔎 Lesson 8: Attention Is All You Need

#### **Question of the Day:** How does a language model determine the meaning of words within a sentence? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson delves into the ambiguity of language and the necessity of context for meaning. Students first discuss examples of ambiguous language, followed by an activity where they brainstorm words with multiple meanings and craft sentences to clarify their intended meanings through context. After trading papers with a partner, students guess the intended meanings, identifying which words provided the most context. Reflecting on this, the lesson connects the activity to how language models use embeddings for word meanings and neural networks for relational meaning, introducing the concept of Attention as the process of updating word meanings based on context. #### **Students will be able to:** - #### Construct examples of words and sentences that require context clues to understand fully - #### Explain how embeddings and neural networks are used in the attention process ### Full Lesson Plan

dsls.markdown:
# Output ## Lesson Highlight: Mad Lib Activity #### During **Lesson 9: Outputs & Probabilities**, students investigate how language models generate output based on probabilities through creating mad-libs together. #### Student groups create a mad-lib using a predetermined set of words. As groups share, you'll type in their responses in the **Mad Lib App** to explore how their choices compare to what others said. You'll have the opportunity to try out the **Mad Lib App** on the next level. #### As you explore computer generated mad-lib answers, you and your students will notice that the most probable responses are often predictable and generic. This is also what tends to happen with real language models - when asked to be creative without special prompting, the results from a language model can be bland and lukewarm since it's pulling from the most commonly used phrases associated with the input

Teacher Tip: There are several sets of cards and half-sheets to manage in this activity. The lesson plan has some material management strategies recommended by teachers who piloted this unit.

## Lesson Previews
🔎 Lesson 9: Outputs & Probabilities

#### **Question of the Day:** What is the last step for language models to generate output responses? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students investigate how language models generate output based on probabilities. The lesson kicks off with the class collaboratively crafting a story, with a twist to alternate between realistic and fantastical elements. This leads into a group activity where students fill in a mad lib with predetermined words, aiming for either coherent or absurd outcomes. They are then introduced to an app that aggregates responses from numerous users to the same mad lib, showcasing a variety of popular choices for each blank. Through selecting the most popular choices based on this data, students experience firsthand how language models select words with the highest probability from training data. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain how a language model uses probabilities to generate words as ouput ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 10: Hallucinations and Fabrications

#### **Question of the Day:** What happens when a language model gives inaccurate information? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students investigate why language models may produce inaccurate outputs, known as hallucinations. They start by understanding the probabilistic nature of generative AI and how bias in training data can lead to such errors. Following discussions on examples of AI systems recalled for bias and hallucinations, students assume the role of AI Auditors to explore proactive measures for mitigating harm caused by deployed language models. After their exploration, they watch a video of a professional AI Auditor, reflecting on the parallels between the professional's work and their class activity. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Make recommendations regarding the input and output of a language model to avoid bias and harm ### Full Lesson Plan

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
# Mad Lib App!

This app corresponds to the Outputs & Probabilities lesson in the Foundations of Generative AI unit.

**Teachers: Click the 'For Teachers Only' text in the header of these instructions to view additional details about this app!**
https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.teacher_markdown:
## Part 1: Generating a Mad-Lib

After students have decided on the words for their mad lib, you can use this screen to generate some of the mad libs and read them as a class. This can be a lighthearted moment in class before continuing.

## Part 2: Viewing the Data & Random Mad-Lib

You can do two things on this screen:

1.  View the data from 1000 peolple who completed the same mad lib, so  you can see all of the different choices they made for filling in the blanks
2.  Randomly view one of those mad-libs

This is important for the next step, where the mad lib generated by the computer uses the "most popular" choices from this data to make its decisions.

## Part 3: Highest Probability Mad-Lib

On this screen, you will use suggestions from the class to generate possibilities for each blank in the mad lib and then the computer will decide which suggestion to use. The computer bases its choice on the "most popular" word from the 1000 person dataset viewed in the last screen. This process emphasizes the idea that even when we have a list of different words that make sense in the context of the sentence, the final decider is based on probability learned from the initial training data.

1.  On the left, enter all of the possible responses the class generated for Noun 1
2.  Click the "Calculate Scores" button. This will compare the list of class possibilities to the dataset representing what 1000 other people submitted for their madlib. The scores will appear next to each word. After reviewing, press the Continue button
3.  The highest score will be chosen, and the top of the screen will update. Then the side-panel will refresh and you can now enter the class possibilities for Noun 2.
4.  Follow the same steps until all of the most popular nouns have been chosen
5.  Once all the nouns have been selected, A Generate Mad Lib button will appear. Press it to generate the mad lib!

At any time, press the "Restart This Screen" button to start this process over

dsls.markdown:
# Lesson 11: Project: Demystifying Generative AI #### In this project, students read several generative AI opinion articles that contain misrepresentations or exaggerations of what generative AI is capable of and how it works. Using their newfound knowledge from this unit, students draft a rebuttal and create an artifact to help visualize or conceptualize an aspect of their argument. This project acts as a summative assessment for the content of this unit. --- ## Project Guide ### Click here for the complete Project Guide or check out the full Lesson Plan.

--- ## Up Next! #### Explore the steps students will go through when completing their projects.

# Navigating this Module #### 🎉 Congratulations on completing this lesson.
📝 Engage with our Learning Community!

####Participating in the Gen AI Self-Paced PL Learning Community is optional and not necessary to earn a certificate for this module. However, we encourage you to engage with the broader Gen AI learner community. #### **Please share how you would explain the concept of Generative AI to a group of students using only emojis.**

#### **What's next?** This professional learning module includes two additional lessons to enhance your understanding and teaching of the Exploring Generative AI course. - #### **Lesson 1: Introduction to Teaching Exploring Generative AI**- This lesson gives an overview of the curriculum features in Exploring Generative AI. - #### **Lesson 2: Foundations of Generative AI** - This lesson covers basic concepts on Generative AI introduced in the first unit of the course. #### ➡️ You are here! - #### **Lesson 3: Customizing Large Language Models** - This lesson covers the second unit in the Exploring Generative AI course and focuses on practicing skills for the hands-on activities that involve exploring, customizing, and creating AI bots. ## Let's continue this exciting journey!

# Customizing Large Language Models #### Now that you have learned more about what Generative AI is, let's dive into how to customize large language models. #### This unit is designed to deepen students' understanding of how text-based generative AI models function and how they can be customized for specific tasks. Students will explore how system prompts and retrieval methods influence the behavior of AI models, allowing them to generate more accurate and relevant responses. Then, students will learn user-centered design strategies to design even more specialized chatbots. #### By the end of this unit, students will have the skills to create a chatbot that addresses a personally relevant issue or an issue in their community. ## **In this lesson you will:** - #### Review the content of each lesson in the Customizing Large Language Models Unit - #### Preview activities in the lessons ### **Suggested time:** 60 minutes

# The PRIMM Method #### The lessons in this unit use the **PRIMM** methodology for learning. The **PRIMM** method is a structured approach to teaching and learning programming. #### It stands for Predict, Run, Investigate, Modify, and Make. As you work through the lessons, you will notice that they follow this approach. ## Here is a breakdown of each step:
🤔Predict

Students are encouraged to first predict what the generative AI model will do. This helps them engage with the logic and structure of the model, fostering a deeper understanding of how it works.

🏃 Run

In this step, students run the model to see if their predictions were correct. This immediate feedback helps reinforce correct predictions and correct misunderstandings.

🔎Investigate

After running the the model, students explore why it behaves as it does by examining its structure, syntax, and logic. They might debug, analyze flow, and identify errors. This step emphasizes comprehension and critical thinking.

🧩Modify

Students change the existing model by altering settings, adding functionality, or fixing errors. This helps them apply their understanding and experiment, reinforcing learning through practice.

💡 Make

Finally, students apply their knowledge to create something new. This step encourages creativity and solidifies their skills.

--- #### The **PRIMM** method is effective because it combines prediction and active experimentation with reflection and creation, promoting a deeper understanding of programming concepts and problem-solving skills. ## Student Responses #### The teaching guide is a great tool to help incorporate student responses in lessons and build engaging discussions in your class in real-time using the tools and features on the Code.org platform, especially during the predict and discuss levels. #### Take a few minutes to look over the teaching guide.

#Exploration, Practice, Synthesis #### The lessons in this unit are scaffolded to follow a clear flow of exploration, practice, and synthesis to effectively teach generative AI concepts: - #### **🔎Exploration:** Each lesson starts with an exploration phase, introducing the foundational concepts and technologies behind generative AI. This phase fosters curiosity and engagement through interactive discussions and demonstrations. - #### **✍️Practice:** earners engage in hands-on activities to apply their knowledge, including experiments with generative models and real-time use of AI tools. - #### **⚙️Synthesis:** The synthesis phase encourages learners to integrate and reflect on what they've learned and connect the dots between theory and practice, solidifying their grasp on generative AI concepts. #### This structured approach ensures that learners gain a comprehensive understanding of generative AI, from theoretical foundations to practical implementation and critical reflection.

# Introducing AI Chat Lab #### **In this unit, students will learn how to use and customize their own chatbot using AI Chat Lab**. #### The chatbots students are working with are in devleopment, and not finished products like ChatGPT or customer support chatbots on a website. So, they need our help to make them better. #### This will be our main goal in this unit - to learn techniques to improve chatbots and models once they've already been created.

Teacher Tip: The first lessons in this unit have great opportunities to make sure students become fluent with using the chatbot. Make sure students know where the instructions panel is, can send messages, know how to clear the chat, and know how to copy the chat. Building this fluency early will make later lessons easier.

## Up Next! #### In the next levels you'll get to try **AI Chat Lab**. Take your time exploring chatbot lab as you and your students will be using this tool for both the remainder of the Generative AI Unit.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions: ## 🔍Teacher Tip In this level, students will attempt to figure out the specific purpose of this chatbot. You may notice frustration when the bot says ‘I’m not interested in that’. Remind them to play the role of a detective as they explore how chatbots work. * * * ## Explore a Chatbot These levels let you experiment with chatbots. They use a language model behind the scenes to answer questions. Let's experiment and see what questions we can get answered. **Fair Warning:** This chatbot is for educational purposes, so we've built in appropriate restrictions and accountability to make sure it doesn't get abused. Inappropriate messages from you _or_ the chatbot will be flagged and not displayed. The chatbot also keeps track of **all messages you send to it**, and your teacher can view these messages (even the ones that were flagged as inappropriate). So be sure to just use the chatbot as directed! ### Do This 1. Ask the chatbot 3-4 questions. Try to determine its purpose. 2. Find the button that lets you clear the chat. This also resets the conversation 3. Find the button that lets you copy the chat. This will be useful to know about later! Test the chatbot! Have a conversation with at least 4 messages, then press the Continue button!   ::: details [**Click here to see example messages to try in this level**] - What is space like? - Where is Tucson located? - Where can I get a good chile relleno? :::

## 🔍Teacher Tip In this level, you will notice the middle segment, ‘Model Customization’. This is the same chatbot as the previous level, but now you can see its purpose in the model card. Encourage your students to carefully read each section of the model card to fully understand how this chatbot works. * * * ## Explore a Chatbot Round 2 This chatbot now has additional information you can view, called the **Model Card**. This displays additional information for you, the user, about how the chatbot works. ### Do This 1. Read the model card to learn more about how the chatbot works 2. With this new information: try to have a successful conversation with the chatbot Test the chatbot! Have a conversation with at least 4 messages, then press the Continue button!

dsls.markdown:
# Safety Note with AI Chat Lab #### You may have noticed this pop-up to when you opened up a level using AI Chat Lab. This message will also be displayed to your students as they work with AI Chat Lab. #### While teaching this Unit, it's important to emphasize responsible use when students interact with AI Chat Lab. Here are a few tips: * #### **Encourage Awareness**: Remind students that all interactions with the chatbot are recorded and monitored, ensuring that their conversations remain respectful and on-topic. Any inappropriate messages will be flagged, and consequences may follow. * #### **Protect Personal Information**: Teach students the importance of keeping personal information private. They should avoid sharing details like their name, phone number, or address when interacting with the chatbot. * #### **Promote Responsible Communication**: Reinforce the idea that the chatbot is a tool for learning and growth, not just for casual conversation. Encourage students to use it responsibly and with a focus on education. * #### **Be Prepared to Have Discussions with Students**: Use unexpected outputs as teaching moments. Encourage critical thinking by discussing potential biases in AI, how AI works, and why the chatbot might generate certain outputs. * #### **Actively Monitor Student Usage**: You should actively monitor student usage to identify any potential issues, providing guidance and intervention as necessary to maintain a safe and supportive learning environment. Learn more about how to do so here.

If you come across a student message that was not flagged but you believe should have been, you can manually flag it as inappropriate. This helps us enhance our safety filter and better protect students.

#### For more information about safety with AI Chat Lab check out the FAQ Support Article. ## Up Next! #### In the next level you'll get to experiment with **temperature**. Temperature is one of the values you can control once a language model has been trained. Experiment with temperature and see if you can tell what it does!

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions: ## 🔍Teacher Tip Don't move on until you can summarize in your own words what temperature does in the model. * * * ## Exploring Temperature **Temperature** is one of the values you can control once a language model has been trained. Let's experiment with it and see if we can tell what it does. ### Do This 1. Have a 3-4 message conversation with the chatbot about a book or movie or band you like. 2. Change the temperature to a high value like 0.8 **Press the Update button!** 3. Have another 3-4 message conversation about the same topic. Do you notice a difference in the type of responses? 4. Change the temperature to a low value like 0.2 **Press the Update button!** 5. Have another 3-4 message conversation about the same topic. Do you notice a difference in the type of responses? **Discuss:** How were the responses different for higher temperatures? Lower temperatures?

answers.text: Temperature affects which words are chosen as a language model tries to generate new words. The higher the temperature, the more random the word choices.
Temperature affects which words are chosen as a language model tries to generate new words. The lower the temperature, the more random the word choices
Temperature changes how nice the chatbot is. The higher the temperature, the nicer the responses.
Temperature changes how nice the chatbot is. The lower the temperature, the nicer the responses.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes what changing temperature does to the responses you get from the chatbot?

dsls.markdown:
# Lessons 1: Interacting with Language Models #### The levels you've been working through come from the first lesson in the Customizing Large Language Models Unit. Click the box below to check-out the lesson plan.
🔎 Lesson 1: Interacting with Language Models

#### **Question of the Day:** How can you continue to improve AI agents once they've been created? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students are presented an overview of the unit and explore the chatbot lab for the first time. Students begin by exploring a chatbot, using a model card to guide their understanding of its capabilities and limitations. Next they learn about the temperature setting in chatbots and how it influences the creativity and reliability of responses. Through hands-on experimentation, students will adjust the temperature to see its effect on the chatbot's answers. Finally, students explore how chatbots can hallucinate to produce incorrect or misleading information. Throughout the unit, students will monitor and document these instances, enhancing their critical awareness of AI's limitations. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain how temperature settings influence the creativity and reliability of chatbot responses * #### Identify and explain hallucinations in chatbot responses ### Full Lesson Plan

## Up Next! #### Learn about **System Prompting** and how we can start to customize large language models.

panels.game_design_spr_types-6451ed33-47e5-458c:
## **System Prompt:**

### You can give additional guidance to a chatbot by typing in a System Prompt
panels.game_design_spr_types-53c92e2f-0462-48a7:
## **System Prompt:**

### When using a system prompt, it can be helpful to include:

-   a specific **goal** that the chatbot is designed to accomplish
-   any **constraints** on how the chatbot should respond
panels.game_design_spr_types-4936be88-574e-4dd8:
## **System Prompt:**

### After setting the system prompt, make sure to press the Update button
panels.gen-ai-panels-test-L1-a74895ac-5363-4d66:
## **System Prompt:**

### You'll see an update in the chatbot window letting you know it was a success.

Then you can start chatting with your new chatbot!

dsls.markdown:
# System Prompting #### There are lots of new key concepts introduced around system prompting that students will engage with during these lessons. We've summarized them below.
Role

#### The part the model plays when responding, like being a teacher, helper, or friend.

Tone

#### The way the model speaks, such as being formal, friendly, or casual.

Audience

#### The people the model is talking to, like kids, adults, or professionals.

Delivery

#### How the model says things, whether it's simple, detailed, quick, or slow, depending on who's listening.

## Up Next! #### The next level is an example of an **Assessment Level** from the curriculum. These levels require students to apply the skills they've learned with less scaffolding and support than other levels. Teachers are encouraged to use these for gathering information about what students are and are not grasping from the lessons. #### But don't worry! We're going to help you out before you try this level. **Teacher answer keys and exemplars are provided as part of each lesson plan.** Before you move on, review the exemplar shown below.


🔍 Where do I find the exemplars?

#### On each relevant Lesson Plan you'll find a link to the Key and Exemplars in the Links, for Teacher's section.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
## 🔍Teacher Tip

Remember to include all components of system prompting - role, tone, audience, and delivery. If you're struggling, go back and review the concepts and exemplars from the last level.

* * *

## Assessment Level

You are designing a chatbot to help a nephew with their homework. The chatbot should focus on answering questions about 6th grade science, and not get distracted with other topics. Design a chatbot to meet these needs

 

::: details [**Click here to see example messages to try in this level**]

-   I need help studying the solar system.
-   Can you explain ecosystems?
    :::

dsls.markdown:
# System Prompting #### There are two more strategies you can use when writing system prompts.
Chain of Reasoning

#### The steps the model takes to explain its answer, showing how it got from the question to the final response, like solving a math problem step by step.

Giving Examples

#### Providing specific instances or situations to make the explanation clearer, like saying, "For example, when you..." to help people understand better.

## Up Next! #### The next level is a **Choice Level** from the curriculum. Choice levels offer a chance to differentiate learning - in this one you'll choose two different scenarios to apply your system prompting skills. #### When you're trying out this level, remember the system prompting strategies we've discussed already - **role, tone, audience, and delivery** - as well as **chain of reasoning and giving examples**. Feel free to come back to this level if you get stuck.
🧐 Hint

#### You can find teacher answer keys and exemplars as part of each lesson plan. This activity is from Lesson 4.

dsls.description: Choose two levels to complete - one using chain of reasoning, and one using examples.

gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9A_.bubble_choice_description: Write a prompt that generates dialogue between two characters using chain of reasoning.
gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9A_.long_instructions: ## Dialogue Generator Jorge is a writer who loves thinking up plot points, or describing the setting, or developing interesting characters... but he doesn't really like writing dialogue. He'd like to create a chatbot that helps him write the dialogue between his characters. He'd like the dialogue to match his story, so the chatbot should take into account as many story details as it can. ### Do this Write a prompt that generates dialogue between two characters. Include at least two steps that ask the user for more details about the story, incorporating details like character traits, potential plot points, or the setting of the story. Test the chatbot! Have a few different conversations, then press the Continue button!

gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9B_.bubble_choice_description: Create a prompt that uses chain of reasoning to recommend whether or not someone should send an email.
gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9B_.long_instructions: ## Should I send the email? Sarah wants to create a chatbot that lets her know whether or not she should send an email. It should analyze the email for several factors (like tone, clarity, etc) before providing an overall recommendation ### Do This Create a prompt that uses chain of reasoning to recommend whether or not someone should send an email. The steps should include different criteria to evaluate the email for, and then combining those results into a single recommendation. Test the chatbot! Type in 3-4 messages and press the Continue button!

gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9C_.bubble_choice_description: Create a chatbot that generates social media posts in the same style and voice as someone recognizable on social media using examples.
gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9C_.long_instructions:
## Social Media Sound-Alike

Create a chatbot that generates social media posts in the same style and voice as someone recognizable on social media. The user can provide a topic and the chatbot responds with a post on that topic.

You should either make up examples in their voice and style, or provide real examples from their social media posts.

gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9D_.bubble_choice_description: Create a chatbot that acts as a "Yes-man" who just mindlessly agrees with everything you say. Use examples to set a specific pattern for how the chatbot should respond.
gen-ai-customizing-prompt-synthesis-L9D_.long_instructions:
## Agreeable friend

Create a chatbot that acts as a "Yes-man" who just mindlessly agrees with everything you say. For example, if you say "the world is ending", the chatbot will say "I agree - the world is ending".

Use examples to make sure your chatbot is responding in a clear agreeable pattern.

 

::: details [**Click here to see example messages to try in this level**]
* The world is ending
* Cats are better than dogs
* A hot dog is a sandwich
:::

dsls.markdown:
# Lessons 2-4: System Prompting #### Now that you have an idea of how **System Prompting** works, take a look the lesson plans associated with this learning concept. These lessons go through the **Exploration, Practice, Synthesis** model described earlier in this module. #### Take as much time as you'd like to review these lessons.
🔎 Lesson 2: System Prompting - Exploration

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we influence how a chatbot behaves? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Explore lesson introduces the system prompt and guides students through an investigation on how it impacts a chatbot's behavior. Students start by predicting and discussing how a system prompt can impact a chatbot's behavior, and then investigate and modify existing chatbots to confirm or expand their initial guesses. Through guided activities, they will learn how the design of a prompt can direct and limit chatbot interactions. Students will practice crafting prompts to elicit specific responses from the AI and engage in activities that incorporate personal experiences to see the effect of their prompt design on how the chatbot functions. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain how system prompts can influence the behavior of a chatbot * #### Modify system prompts to achieve desired chatbot responses ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 3: System Prompting - Practice

#### **Question of the Day:** How do we write system prompts to meet specific needs? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Practice lesson builds fluency in designing and applying system prompts, expanding the knowledge students developed in the previous lesson. Through examples and guided practice, students learn to craft prompts that guide not only the content but also the style and appropriateness of AI responses based on different audiences and situations. Students apply these skills through practice scenarios simulating real-life use cases and user-stories, emphasizing how problem-solving with system prompts can be applied to address real-world issues. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Refine system prompts by considering role, tone, audience, and delivery modality * #### Write system prompts that meet specific user needs ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 4: System Prompting - Synthesis

#### **Question of the Day:** What are more complex strategies for system prompting? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Synthesis lesson expands on the prompting techniques students have learned in the previous two lessons, and invites them to revisit and reflect on how their skills have grown. Students explore two advanced prompting techniques: (1) chain of reasoning, which helps chatbots deliver more precise and relevant responses by giving explicit steps, and (2) providing examples, which enhances the chatbot's accuracy and context appropriateness. After practicing these skills in specific contexts, students revisit and refine earlier lesson activities using the knowledge they've accumulated so far. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Use chain-of-reasoning techniques to create system prompts for complex tasks * #### Use examples to guide chatbots in generating accurate and specific responses ### Full Lesson Plan

Teacher Tip: As students struggle getting the chatbot to respond in a particular way, they may want to know the “trick” or “secret” to getting the chatbot to respond how they want. You can assure students that there is no trick, just a set of strategies that tend to work more often than others but are still unreliable.


📝 Engage with our Learning Community!

#### Participating in the Gen AI Self-Paced PL Learning Community is optional and not necessary to earn a certificate for this module. However, we encourage you to engage with the broader Gen AI learner community. #### **If you were to build a chatbot using AI Chat Lab to support your teaching practice, what key development considerations would you want to keep in mind?**

## Up Next! #### You'll learn about the next tool in our AI tool box - **retrieval**.

https://studio.code.org/s/self-paced-pl-.long_instructions:
#### Using **retrieval**, we can provide the chatbot with different types of information to help it respond more accurately. In general we will want to provide information that is... - #### **Current**: Chatbots only have information up to a certain date. For more recent information, we can add it with retrieval. - #### **Specialized**: Chatbots tend to have general information about topics. For more specialized information, we can add it with retrieval - #### **Accurate**: Chatbots don't have all the answers and sometimes hallucinate. For situations that require very high accuracy, we can add information with retrieval.

Teacher Tip: Students will eventually be encouraged to spend their time searching for sources and copying directly from them, rather than trying to write retrieval information themselves. This correlates to how retrieval works in a more realistic sense with actual language models: instead of looking at individual words or statements, modern large-language models ingest entire documents or manuals or websites in order to augment their knowledgebase.

## **Up Next!** #### Try out applying **retrieval** in AI Chat Lab. Watch the video below first to learn how to do so.

## 🔍Teacher Tip

This level is a good opportunity to practice retrieval and to hone your system prompting skills. Take your time and make sure to test your chatbot thoroughly.

Remember to switch to the retrieval tab to add in the required information. Make sure you update your model before testing!

* * *

## Restaurant Chatbot

Lin is making a chatbot for her family's restaurant, [Sky Dragon][0]. She thinks the chatbot will be a good option for people who may not want to fully browse the website - it could pop up in the corner and say "Can I help you find something great to eat?". Talking to the chatbot should feel like talking to someone who visits the restaurant all the time and is super excited by the menu.

To make this work, Lin needs to add specific information from her restaurant website to the chatbot. This should include:

-   The name, description, and price of at least 3 dishes
-   The address and phone number of the restaurant
-   A general description of the type of food served at the restaurant

Help Lin create this chatbot by adding a system prompt and retrieval! You should have at least 8 pieces of retrieval in the chatbot!

**Optional Adjustment:** Instead of making a chatbot for this restaurant, you could make one for a different local restaurant in your city. The restaurant still needs a website to pull information from, and will still need the items listed above. Once you've confirmed this information is on the restaurant website, ask your teacher and make sure it's okay to change the assignment on this level!

dsls.markdown:
# Lessons 5-7: Retrieval #### Lessons 5 through 7 help students develop an understanding of **retrieval**. Take some time to review these lesson plans. ## Lesson Overviews
🔎 Lesson 5: Retrieval - Exploration

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we update the information in a chatbot? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Explore lesson introduces the concept of retrieval and guides students through an investigation on how it impacts a chatbot's behavior. Students start by predicting and discussing how retrieval can impact a chatbot's behavior, and then investigate and modify existing chatbots to confirm or expand their initial guesses. Through guided activities, they will learn how adding specific information to a chatbot's knowledge base can significantly improve the accuracy and relevance of its responses. Students will practice adding retrieval information to elicit specific responses from the AI and engage in activities that incorporate personal experiences to see the effect of their retrieval on how the chatbot functions. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain how retrieval allows chatbots to access updated information * #### Modify chatbot responses by adding and removing retrieval statements ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 6: Retrieval - Practice

#### **Question of the Day:** When is retrieval most useful in solving a problem? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Practice lesson builds fluency in designing and applying retrieval statements, expanding the knowledge students developed in the previous lesson. Through examples and guided practice, students explore how retrieval can be used to provide current, specialized, and accurate information, helping to reduce the occurrence of AI hallucinations. Students apply these skills through practice scenarios simulating real-life use cases and user-stories, emphasizing the use of trusted real-world data sources (ie: copy-and-pasting accurate sentences) is an effective strategy to address real-world issues. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Identify scenarios where retrieval is essential for updating chatbot information * #### Use retrieval to provide current, specialized, and accurate information to chatbots ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 7: Retrieval - Synthesis

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we use retrieval and system prompting strategically to solve problems? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This Synthesis lesson focuses on applying retrieval and system prompting to three complex user scenarios. Students begin by brainstorming and planning how to use these strategies to achieve user goals effectively. They will also explore methods for testing and evaluating chatbots to prevent potential harms. After planning, students will design chatbots tailored to each scenario, deciding strategically between using system prompts or retrieval. The lesson concludes with a reflection on the appropriate use of retrieval versus system prompting and revisits previous tasks, allowing students to apply their new insights to enhance their earlier work. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Differentiate when to use retrieval versus system prompting to address specific chatbot tasks * #### Strategically combine retrieval and system prompting to solve complex problems with chatbots ### Full Lesson Plan

Teacher Tip: There are some opportunities in these lessons to tie in the retrieval process to embeddings which were covered into the Foundations of Generative AI Unit.

# Model Cards #### Model cards contain additional information about the chatbot for the user to review. #### These can include safety features which help the user know how to use the chatbot responsibly. Or transparency information, so the user knows how the chatbot was created or designed. And even usability information, which helps the user know where to get started. #### You first saw model cards at the beginning of this unit when trying out the Recipe Recommender chatbot. Take a moment to review it now.

Teacher Tip: Students may feel challenged when asked to write, explain, and reflect on their chatbots after earlier, more hands-on lessons. Acknowledge this shift but encourage them to embrace it, as documenting and describing digital creations is crucial. Remind them that this task requires a different skill set but is equally important. Provide writing support, like having students explain their thoughts verbally before writing them down.

## Lesson Overviews
🔎 Lesson 8: Model Cards - Exploration

#### **Question of the Day:** What information should we be providing to potential users of our chatbots? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students are introduced to Model Cards and Foresight in AI charts as tools used to plan for how AI products are used. Students begin by reflecting on what materials are provided to users when we buy a new product, then explore how a Model Card serves a similar purpose for chatbots to clarify and caution how a particular chatbot product should and shouldn't be used. Then, students explore an example of a Library chatbot and use a Foresight in AI table to examine the intended and unintended users and use-cases for this chatbot. After this example, students create their own model card for a fictitious chatbot, and then analyze each other's model cards with a Foresight in AI chart. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Analyze and create model cards for chatbots, including potential intended and unintended uses * #### Explain the importance of providing safety, transparency, and usability information for chatbots through model cards ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 9: Model Cards - Practice

#### **Question of the Day:** How can a Model Card help document decisions when making a chatbot? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students explore how to create model cards as they develop their chatbots. Students start by helping a friend design a chatbot to give more specific recommendations using a series of multiple-choice focus questions. Once these decisions are made, students document these decisions on the model card for the chatbot, emphasizing how a model card can also serve as documentation as you make decisions during chatbot development. After doing an example as a class, students choose a new scenario and repeat this process. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Create and document model cards for chatbots, including making strategic decisions about their behavior ### Full Lesson Plan

# Project: Create a Personal Chatbot #### In the first project project of this unit, students **create a chatbot that represents their personality and is an expert on a specific interest they care about**. #### Using their newfound knowledge from this unit, students apply techniques to adjust the temperature, system prompt, and retrieval settings of a chatbot. After testing their chatbot, they create a model card and “publish” it so other users can interact with it. --- ## Project Guide ### Click here for the complete Project Guide or check out the full Lesson Plan. --- ## Gallery Walk #### Consider adding an collaborative opportunity for your class with an Optional **Gallery Walk** at the end of this project. This step allows students to showcase their chatbots in **User View** providing an opportunity for their peers to interact with and explore each other's work. By participating in this gallery walk, students can gain valuable insights and inspiration from their classmates. #### Encourage your students to leave constructive feedback using post-it notes, starting with phrases like “I like…” or “I wish…”. This not only fosters a positive and supportive learning environment but also helps students develop their ability to give and receive feedback—an essential skill in both academic and professional settings. While this step is optional, it can significantly enhance the collaborative atmosphere in your classroom, making the learning experience more enriching for everyone.

# User-Centered Design #### For the remainder of this unit students develop their skills related to **User-Centered Design** focusing on building chatbots that are not only functional, but also useful for users in their specific situations. #### The lessons in this portion of the unit will help students develop their skills to complete the **Final Project, Solving Community Problems with Chatbots**. ## Lesson Overviews
🔎 Lesson 11: Issue Statements

#### **Question of the Day:** How can generative AI be used to address an issue in your community? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students explore a design thinking strategy to help identify core issues in their community and brainstorm ways generative AI can help solve them. Students start with an initial observation of a problem in their community, then use the 5 Why's strategy to identify the larger core issue that could be addressed. Students then use model cards to brainstorm possible solutions, then discuss four possible solutions provided by other students. These activities act as a “practice run” for skills and processes students will do for their project later in the unit. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Brainstorm chatbot solutions to address identified community issues * #### Identify community issues through observation and empathy ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 12: User Feedback

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we test our chatbots with a variety of users to verify they work as expected and avoid unintended harm? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### This lesson has two central parts - first students explore how to act as user testers for a chatbot app, then students help decide what the next-steps should be for a chatbot based on the testing data. This lesson builds to the project students will complete at the end of the unit, where they will user-test each other's apps using the same structure in this lesson. Students also wrestle with the responsible use of AI and help decide whether or not a chatbot should be launched publicly even when some flaws are uncovered, emphasizing how humans remain decision-makers for how AI products impact society. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Explain the importance of user testing in developing AI applications * #### Make informed decisions based on user feedback to improve chatbot performance and usability * #### Perform user testing on chatbots and provide constructive feedback ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 13: Fine Tuning - Exploration

#### **Question of the Day:** How can we make deep changes to chatbots that affect it's core behavior? #### **Lesson Overview:** #### In this lesson, students are introduced to the process of fine-tuning as a process for altering the core behavior of a language model. The lesson starts with students reflecting on how inside jokes or slang expressions create connections that are difficult to explain to folks outside the group - they must be experienced to be understood. This idea ties into the lesson as we explore a new chatbot that has been specifically designed to act as a medical expert. Students observe how speaking to the medical expert chatbot is different from the typical chatbot due to how the both has been fine-tuned on additional medical data. Students learn about the fine-tuning process, then explore additional models on the Hugging Face website to see additional fine-tuned examples. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Experiment with different fine-tuned models to observe how additional data sources influence chatbot responses * #### Explain the concept of fine-tuning and how it affects the core behavior of chatbots ### Full Lesson Plan

🔎 Lesson 14: Chatbots in Society

#### **Lesson Overview:** #### Students explore the impact of generative AI by participating in a collaborative game. Students take on a persona of someone impacted by generative AI to read a few articles and react in-character. Then, students form groups and are given a scenario involving their persona and generative AI. Working together, they come up with guidelines for how generative AI should be implemented in this scenario that includes the voices and perspectives of all stakeholders. Finally, students share their results with the class and reflect on this process. #### **Students will be able to:** * #### Collaboratively develop guidelines for the use of generative AI that consider multiple stakeholders' needs and concerns * #### Understand different perspectives on the impact of generative AI in a specific scenario ### Full Lesson Plan

## Up Next! #### Explore the final project for the Customizing Language Models Unit.

# Final Project: Solving Community Problems with Chatbots #### In the final project of this curriculum, **students create a chatbot that addresses a personally relevant issue or an issue in their community**. #### Students rely on the user-centered design strategies they've seen throughout the unit to create issue statements, design a chatbot, perform user testing, and reflect on the results. Optionally, students can design a presentation on their project and the problem it was designed to address.

Teacher Tip: Your students will explore one of three example projects before starting the project. You can check them out yourselves here.

However, we encourage you to engage with the broader Gen AI learner community. #### **Please share what chatbot you would develop if you were tasked with completing this project.**

--- ## The Design Thinking Process #### In this project students will work through the **Design Thinking Process**. The process involves five steps that teams use to understand users, challenge assumptions, redefine problems, and create innovative solutions. ### Cyclical Design Process #### Students will use this process throughout this unit as they work towards developing a prototype for a computer vision product. It involves understanding users, defining problems, brainstorming solutions, creating prototypes, and testing them while emphasizing a **user-centric mindset**. ### Why use this process? #### By practicing these steps, students will learn to continuously improve their ideas and ultimately develop their own prototypes. This cyclical process helps students build creativity and problem-solving skills in a practical, hands-on way. ## Up Next! #### Explore the steps students will go through when completing their projects.
